speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Today we're working with one of those GitHub features that quietly makes open source collaboration much easier: issue templates.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And this is a great one for people who have ever opened a blank issue form and thought, what exactly am I supposed to put here?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. An issue template turns that blank box into a guided experience. Instead of guessing what maintainers need, contributors get prompts, structure, and clear required fields. That reduces back-and-forth, improves quality, and helps teams act on issues faster.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is not just about formatting. It's about making contributions clearer and more usable.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, and especially in accessibility work. If someone is reporting an accessibility barrier, a vague report can be hard to reproduce. A thoughtful template can ask where the problem occurred, what assistive technology was involved, what the expected behavior was, and how severe the barrier is.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we get into the design challenge, what should learners already have in place?

speaker: ALEX
text: A few practical things. First, you should already know how to work with GitHub issues at a basic level. Second, you need a repository where you can create or edit files, usually your fork or your own repo. Third, it really helps to use an editor with YAML syntax highlighting, such as VS Code.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And some things are recommended but not strictly required, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Familiarity with GitHub Copilot can help if you want to generate variations, but it's optional. Basic terminal comfort is useful, though you can also use the GitHub web editor or github.dev. And if you already filled out the workshop registration template earlier in this course, that template becomes a learning example now.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that. You've already used a professional issue template, so now you reverse-engineer it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Also, there's an administrative note here: this chapter is meant to prepare you for a later workflow using a template-building agent. The advice is to complete the manual template chapter first, then use the automation later.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And time-wise?

speaker: ALEX
text: Plan roughly two to two and a half hours if you do the exercises and some YAML troubleshooting.

speaker: ALEX
text: The learning pattern for this challenge is analyze, remix, create, and test.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels approachable. First understand an existing template, then adapt one, then optionally build another version from scratch, then verify it works.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's the idea. The workshop recommendation frames Challenge 14 as design-focused rather than automation-focused. You're producing evidence of understanding, often as an issue comment or a pull request, rather than trying to satisfy a complicated code runtime.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Though there is still one small autograded requirement in the challenge template itself.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. For the assigned challenge issue, the autograder checks that a YAML file exists in the repository's `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/` folder and that it contains a valid `name` field. But pedagogically, the real goal is broader: understanding how structured templates guide contributions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's define the basic concept clearly. What is an issue template?

speaker: ALEX
text: An issue template is a reusable structure GitHub offers when someone clicks New Issue. It can be a Markdown template or a YAML form-based template. Both help contributors provide the right information. The difference is in how structured and interactive they are.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So a Markdown template is more like a prewritten document with headings and instructions.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. It usually opens a text area containing sections like Problem, Context, Steps to Reproduce, or Additional Context. A YAML form template is more interactive. It creates actual fields like text inputs, dropdowns, textareas, and checkboxes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for accessibility, the YAML form often wins.

speaker: ALEX
text: Often, yes. A good YAML form can make navigation more predictable, clearly indicate required fields, constrain answers when appropriate, and reduce cognitive load. It gives contributors helpful labels and descriptions instead of asking them to infer the structure.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do templates work on GitHub at the repository level?

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub looks in a special folder: `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/`. Files there can appear in the issue template chooser. If they're YAML form templates, they typically use the `.yml` extension. Markdown templates use `.md` and include frontmatter.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And when someone clicks New Issue, GitHub may show a template picker.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. In that chooser, the template's name appears as the visible option, and the description appears below it as helper text. Once the user selects it, GitHub generates the issue form or prefilled issue body.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there's also repository-level configuration, like `config.yml`, though this lesson seems more focused on individual template files.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The chapter mentions configuration and the chooser behavior, including that some setups can change how templates are presented. It also notes there are ways users may bypass the chooser in some situations, so maintainers should think of templates as guidance, not a magical enforcement system.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's useful. Templates improve the default path, even if they don't control every path.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's ground this in the challenge. The challenge issue itself is actually built from a YAML template. That's a teaching move.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the assignment is also an example of the thing you're learning.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. In the challenge template, the top-level keys are doing important work. There's a `name`, which is shown in the chooser. There's a `description`, which tells the user what the template is for. There's a `title`, which pre-fills the issue title using a pattern. In the challenge it includes the username token so the issue title can identify the learner. There are `labels`, which are automatically applied to issues created from that template. And there's a `body`, which defines the form fields themselves.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That mirrors the solution reference too.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. The solution file gives a complete example: an accessibility report template. The comments in that example are especially instructive because they annotate each part.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's unpack those top-level keys one by one, because these are must-know.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, `name`. This is what someone sees in the template chooser after clicking New Issue. It should be short, clear, and specific enough that a contributor knows when to use it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Like "Accessibility Report."

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Second, `description`. This appears below the name in the chooser and gives one sentence of context, like "Report an accessibility barrier in workshop materials."

speaker: JAMIE
text: Third, `labels`.

speaker: ALEX
text: Those are automatically applied to the issue. In the example, labels like `accessibility` and `bug` categorize the issue immediately. That helps triage.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Fourth, `title`.

speaker: ALEX
text: That pre-fills the issue title with a pattern, such as `[A11y]: `. The contributor then finishes the title rather than starting from scratch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And fifth, the big one: `body`.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The body is the definition of the form fields. This is where you choose input types and specify labels, descriptions, placeholders, and validation rules.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now I want to zoom in on the anatomy of a field, because that's where YAML can feel intimidating.

speaker: ALEX
text: A field usually starts with a `type`. Then it often includes an `id`, which is an internal identifier not typically shown to the user. Inside `attributes`, you define what the user experiences: the visible label, the description, placeholder text, and sometimes options. Then under `validations`, you can declare whether the field is required.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So `id` is for the template structure, while `label` is for the human filling it out.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And that distinction is important. Good labels should be plain language and user-centered. Good IDs should be stable, concise, and meaningful to the template author.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's cover the field types. The source is very explicit here.

speaker: ALEX
text: There are five primary field types highlighted. First, `markdown`. This is display-only text. It's not an input field. You use it for instructions, context, reminders, or notices. In the example template, a markdown block thanks the reporter and explains why their feedback matters. Second, `input`. That's a single-line text field. Use it for short answers like file names, URLs, versions, or a specific location where a problem occurred. Third, `textarea`. That's a multi-line text area for longer responses, such as describing the barrier, outlining steps to reproduce, or adding context. Fourth, `dropdown`. This presents predefined options, like assistive technology type or severity. It reduces ambiguity and makes responses easier to sort and compare. Fifth, `checkboxes`. These allow multiple selections and are good for acknowledgments, checklists, or multi-select categories.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the key design question is not just what fields exist, but when to use each one.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. If the answer should be brief and unique, use `input`. If it requires explanation, use `textarea`. If the answer should come from a controlled set, use `dropdown`. If users may need to select several items, use `checkboxes`. And if you want to communicate without collecting data, use `markdown`.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can we walk through the accessibility report example from the solution?

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. It begins with a markdown block that sets a respectful tone and explains why reporting accessibility barriers matters. That kind of context can make reporting feel safer and more purposeful. Then there's an input field asking where the barrier occurred. The label is clear. The description explains what kinds of answers are acceptable, like a file name, URL, or description of the location. A placeholder gives an example. Next comes a required textarea: describe the barrier. The prompt combines what happened with what the user expected to happen. That encourages useful, comparative reporting. Then there's a dropdown for assistive technology, with options like NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver, screen magnifier, voice control, switch access, keyboard only, Other, or Not applicable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That option list is a subtle accessibility win, because it acknowledges different toolchains and avoids assuming everyone uses the same setup.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And then another dropdown captures severity: blocked, difficult but workable, or minor. That helps maintainers prioritize.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the structure is not just neat. It captures information maintainers actually need.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. That's why templates matter.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The chapter also compares Markdown templates and YAML templates. Let's make that concrete.

speaker: ALEX
text: A Markdown template is simpler and more manual. It uses YAML frontmatter at the top, then plain Markdown sections below. One important detail: in Markdown templates, the frontmatter uses `about`, not `description`.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a small syntax difference that could trip people up.

speaker: ALEX
text: Definitely. A Markdown template might start with frontmatter like name, about, title, and labels, wrapped in triple-dash lines. Then the body might include sections such as Problem, Context, and Solution Ideas, often with HTML comments giving instructions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the user sees headings and writes under them, but there are no typed controls like dropdown menus.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. Markdown templates are useful when you want flexibility, when the structure is lightweight, or when a form would be overkill. YAML forms are better when you want consistency, stronger guidance, built-in required fields, and a more structured reporting experience.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The chapter also calls out when to choose each.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Use Markdown when a looser format is acceptable or when you want maximum compatibility and simplicity. Use YAML forms when you want better structure, clearer required input, and a more accessible guided workflow for frequent issue patterns.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also a very practical list of common Markdown bug-report sections in the source. Can we include those?

speaker: ALEX
text: We should. Typical bug-report sections include Describe the Bug, Steps to Reproduce, Expected Behavior, Actual Behavior, Environment, and Additional Context.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for accessibility-focused reporting, there are even more specific fields.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes: Component Affected, Your Assistive Technology Setup, Expected Behavior, Actual Behavior, Steps to Reproduce, WCAG Success Criterion if known, Additional Context, and Before Submitting.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That "if known" phrasing matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. It invites expert detail without making novices feel excluded. Good accessibility templates welcome useful specificity without requiring people to speak in standards language.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the chapter also emphasizes accessibility considerations for Markdown templates.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Because Markdown templates are more manual, instructions must be especially clear. Use meaningful headings, concise prompts, and plain language. Avoid burying essential instructions in large blocks of text. Think about screen reader navigation and cognitive load.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about actually creating files? The chapter includes both browser and VS Code workflows.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. For Markdown templates, you can create them manually in the repository browser or in VS Code. File naming conventions matter because the file must live in `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/`, and the extension determines the kind of template GitHub recognizes. YAML form templates should use `.yml`. Markdown templates use `.md`.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And indentation matters a lot in YAML.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. YAML is sensitive to indentation. Use spaces, typically two spaces per level, and do not use tabs. Also make sure colons are followed by a space where appropriate. These tiny syntax details are often the difference between a working template and a broken one.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's go through the guided challenge sequence, because it's a big part of the chapter.

speaker: ALEX
text: Challenge 14.1 is analysis. You open the existing workshop registration template on GitHub and study it. The goal is to identify the frontmatter fields: `name`, `description`, `title`, and `labels`. Then you inspect the body and notice the field types used there: markdown, input, dropdown, and textarea.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And you also inspect field metadata.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. For each field, identify the `id`, the visible `label`, the explanatory `description`, any `placeholder`, and whether the field is required. One specific prompt in the chapter is to search the registration template for the word "accessible" and notice that the template explicitly mentions screen reader compatibility. That models professional accessibility thinking.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the final reflection is to compare this with a blank issue.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. A blank issue gives you one giant text box. A template gives structure, guidance, and clarity about what matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: Challenge 14.2 is the remix. You take the structure of the registration template and adapt it for a new purpose.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The chapter gives some example contexts: bug report, event attendance, product research, accessibility audit request.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The instruction is to keep the YAML skeleton. That means keep the overall structure: `name`, `description`, `title`, `labels`, and `body`. Keep the internal field pattern too: `type`, `id`, `attributes`, and `validations`.

speaker: JAMIE
text: But change the content.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Change the context-specific parts: labels, visible field names, descriptions, placeholders, and dropdown options. And rethink which fields should be required.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then validate the YAML.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The chapter explicitly suggests using a YAML validator like yamllint.com. Fix any errors, then commit and push the template file.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the "done" condition is a committed remixed template that validates.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Challenge 14.3 is optional and switches formats.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. You create a Markdown-based issue template from scratch. The instructions say to create a new file in `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/` with an `.md` extension, add YAML frontmatter, and then add a few sections with HTML comment guidance.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And again, the frontmatter detail matters: Markdown templates use `about` instead of `description`.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. That distinction is easy to miss.

speaker: ALEX
text: Challenge 14.4 is also optional: test your template in the template chooser. Go to your repository on GitHub, activate New Issue, make sure your template appears, then open it and file a test issue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That test is important because syntax can be valid but the overall behavior can still be wrong if the file is misplaced or named incorrectly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Testing verifies the real user experience.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The challenge issue file itself includes a minimum YAML structure. Can we restate that as a checklist?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. At minimum, your challenge template should include: - a `name` field, - a `description` field, - a `title` field using a prefix pattern, - and at least one `textarea` or `input` field in the `body`.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the topic is flexible.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very flexible. The challenge explicitly suggests possibilities such as an accessibility feedback form, workshop improvement suggestion, bug report for a specific tool, or a structured feature request. The solution reference reinforces that almost any topic works: bug report, feature request, session evaluation, help request, and so on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the point is not to copy one exact template. It's to show you understand the pattern.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's make the "what matters" part explicit, because learners often worry they need the exact same fields as the example.

speaker: ALEX
text: The solution says the learning objective is understanding structured issue templates as a way to guide contributors. If your YAML file has a `name`, a `description`, and at least one `body` field, you've met the essential learning target for the challenge.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's reassuring.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Precision matters, but creativity is allowed. Alternate valid templates are expected.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The chapter also spends time on navigating the template picker. What should audio learners understand there?

speaker: ALEX
text: When you activate New Issue in a repository with templates, GitHub may present a list of template choices. Screen reader users should expect to navigate through each template option, hear the template name and usually its descriptive text, and then activate the associated action such as Get started.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the chapter specifically includes a screen reader-oriented hands-on exercise.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. In Exercise A, learners are told to go to the Accessibility Agents repository issues section, open the template chooser, inspect it with their screen reader, select a template, navigate the form fields, fill in the form, preview before submitting, submit the issue, and then reflect on the experience.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That reflection step matters for accessibility learning.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very much. You're not just clicking through. You're evaluating whether the structure supports efficient, understandable input.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can we cover the broader hands-on activities too, even if the challenge focus is issue templates?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, because the source explicitly includes them and we shouldn't omit that administrative and workflow context. Exercise B has learners add the accessibility bug report template to their own fork. The steps are: clone your fork, open it in VS Code, navigate to the templates folder, create a new template file, paste in the accessibility template YAML, verify syntax, create a branch and commit, test the template on GitHub web, test again with your screen reader, and optionally merge your branch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a complete local-to-web workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Then Exercise C is about submitting the work upstream as a pull request. You verify the template is ready, create the PR, write a strong title and description, review before submitting, submit it, respond to feedback, and celebrate the contribution.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the PR description itself is structured too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The source gives prompts like: What does this PR do? Why is this useful? How was this tested? Related issue. That mirrors the general principle we've been teaching: structure helps reviewers too, not just issue reporters.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That connects nicely to the chapter's section on pull request templates.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. The chapter includes pull request templates as a parallel concept. PR templates can ask for Description, Type of Change, How to Test, Accessibility Considerations, and Related Issues.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So templates are part of a larger idea: guiding contribution quality across the workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Issue templates improve incoming reports. PR templates improve proposed changes. Both reduce ambiguity and make collaboration easier.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also a section on building an accessibility bug report template. We've touched that, but what makes that template accessible according to the chapter?

speaker: ALEX
text: Several things. It asks for concrete, relevant information without overwhelming the user. It uses clear labels and descriptions. It includes assistive technology setup, which is crucial context in accessibility debugging. It gives structured severity choices to support prioritization. And it balances required and optional fields so users can provide enough detail without facing an exhausting form.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there's a second example too, a feature request form.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. That signals that the same design principles apply beyond bug reporting. Once you understand the field types and the top-level structure, you can design templates for many recurring contribution patterns in your own projects.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's talk about designing your own project template, because that's one of the final exercises.

speaker: ALEX
text: The chapter breaks that into practical parts: choose your project, identify recurring issue patterns, decide which fields are required, decide which are optional, write placeholders and help text, test locally, optionally deploy to GitHub and test with another person, then reflect on the design.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That reflection step is interesting again.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Template design is user experience design. After testing, ask: Did contributors know what to enter? Were the field labels understandable? Did the required fields collect enough information without creating friction? Was the flow comfortable with a screen reader?

speaker: JAMIE
text: We should also cover common problems and troubleshooting, because the challenge template includes a specific "If you get stuck" table.

speaker: ALEX
text: Good point. Common problem one: YAML syntax error. The recommended fix is to check indentation carefully, use two spaces rather than tabs, and make sure colons are followed by spaces. Problem two: the template does not appear in the issue chooser. Check that the file is in `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/`, has a `.yml` extension if it's a YAML form template, and includes a `name` field. Problem three: you don't know what topic to choose. The advice is simple: any structured form works. You can build a feedback form, recipe, book review, help request, accessibility feedback form, or bug report. Problem four: you finished but want to verify your approach. Use the reference solution.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the troubleshooting advice is both technical and motivational.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What are the expected overall outcomes for this chapter?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learners should understand template structure, including YAML frontmatter concepts, field types, and validation. They should be able to analyze and remix professional templates. They should be able to create both YAML form and Markdown templates. They should understand why templates improve contribution quality. And they should see how templates reduce maintainer effort by guiding contributors toward complete, actionable reports.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a strong payoff for what initially looks like just a formatting lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. This is contribution design.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's return to the specific challenge evidence, because learners often need that exact administrative step.

speaker: ALEX
text: To complete Challenge 14, you open your assigned challenge issue and post a completion comment. The chapter gives a sample format including whether you analyzed the registration template, what context you remixed it for, whether the YAML validates, where the template was committed, whether you created the optional Markdown template, and whether you tested it in the chooser. Then you close the challenge issue when done.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So there are two outputs: the actual template file in your repository, and the evidence comment in the assigned challenge issue.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we wrap, can you give a compact mental model learners can carry into VS Code right now?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sure. Think of a YAML issue template as having two layers. Layer one is chooser metadata: - `name` is what the contributor sees. - `description` explains when to use it. - `title` gives a starting pattern. - `labels` classify the issue automatically. Layer two is the form itself: - `body` contains fields. - Each field has a `type`. - Many fields also have an `id`, visible `label`, supportive `description`, optional `placeholder`, and `validations` such as `required: true`. Then ask yourself three design questions: - What information do maintainers need? - What structure will make that information easier to provide? - How can I make the form clear and accessible for someone using assistive technology?

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's excellent.

speaker: ALEX
text: One final note on sources. The chapter points learners to official GitHub Docs and the GitHub Changelog as the authoritative references for current template behavior. That matters because GitHub's interface and features can evolve.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if a label changes in the UI or a docs example shifts, trust the official docs for the latest details.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: All right. Your next move is simple: inspect a real template, remix one for your own purpose, validate the YAML, test it in GitHub, and submit your evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: And as you do, remember what you're really learning: not just how to write YAML, but how to design a contribution path that is clear, respectful, and accessible.

speaker: JAMIE
text: See you next time.
